message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To determine whether UVA exposure might be associated with COVID-19 deaths</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Ecological regression, with replication in two other countries and pooled estimation</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    2,474 counties of the contiguous USA, 6,755 municipalities in Italy, 6,274 small areas in England. Only small areas in their ‘Vitamin D winter’ (monthly mean UV
                    <jats:sub>vitd</jats:sub>
                    of under 165 KJ/m
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    ) from Jan to April 2020.
                  </jats:p>
                  <jats:p>Participants</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>The ‘at-risk’ population is the total small area population, with measures to incorporate spatial infection into the model. The model is adjusted for potential confounders including long-term winter temperature and humidity.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Main outcome measures</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We derive UVA measures for each area from remote sensed data and estimate their relationship with COVID-19 mortality with a random effect for States, in a multilevel zero-inflated negative binomial model. In the USA and England death certificates had to record COVID-19. In Italy excess deaths in 2020 over expected from 2015-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Data sources</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Satellite derived mean daily UVA dataset from Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency. Data on deaths compiled by Center for Disease Control (USA), Office for National Statistics (England) and Italian Institute of Statistics.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Daily mean UVA (January-April 2020) varied between 450 to 1,000 KJ/m
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    across the three countries. Our fully adjusted model showed an inverse correlation between UVA and COVID-19 mortality with a Mortality Risk Ratio (MRR) of 0.71 (0.60 to 0.85) per 100KJ/m
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    increase UVA in the USA, 0.81 (0.71 to 0.93) in Italy and 0.49 (0.38 to 0.64) in England. Pooled MRR was 0.68 (0.52 to 0.88).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Our analysis, replicated in 3 independent national datasets, suggests ambient UVA exposure is associated with lower COVID-19 specific mortality. This effect is independent of vitamin D, as it occurred at irradiances below that likely to induce significant cutaneous vitamin D3 synthesis. Causal interpretations must be made cautiously in observational studies. Nonetheless this study suggests strategies for reduction of COVID-19 mortality.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Ultraviolet A Radiation and COVID-19 Deaths in the USA with replication studies in England and Italy

reference.unstructured: Lee-Taylor J , Madronich S. Climatology of UV-A, UV-B, and Erythemal Radiation at the Earth’s Surface, 1979-2000. In: DIVISION AC, ed., 2007.
Atmospheric Composition Analysis Group [Available from: http://fizz.phys.dal.ca/~atmos/martin/?page_id=140 accessed 15/05/2020 2020.

Idaho Uo . GRIDMET: University of Idaho Gridded Surface Meteorological Dataset 2020 [Available from: https://developers.google.com/earth-engine/datasets/catalog/IDAHO_EPSCOR_GRIDMET accessed 15/05/2020 2020.
